interrogator_dossier:
# G-III Apparel Group (GIII) — Forensic Dossier

*Interrogator | deep_value | Consumer Cyclical | transcripts FY2025-Q4 (Mar-2025) → FY2026-Q4 (Mar-2026), 5 calls*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story across these five quarters is a single, remarkably consistent transformation thesis: **replace a dying licensed-brand annuity (Calvin Klein + Tommy Hilfiger, licensed from PVH) with owned brands (DKNY, Donna Karan, Karl Lagerfeld, Vilebrequin) before the annuity runs out.** The arc is one of *managed disclosure of a forced amputation, dressed as a strategic choice.*

- **Q4-FY25 (Mar-2025) — "we chose this."** The framing is offensive and proud: record non-GAAP EPS of $4.42 (+9%), owned brands "just over half" of sales, four new launches (Nautica, Halston, Champion, Donna Karan relaunch). CK/Tommy is described as falling from ">50% two years ago" to "~34%," with a *planned* glide to "~25% by end of fiscal 2026." The Donna Karan relaunch is the hero — "single biggest launch... we've ever tackled," "$1 billion... potential," "approaches 40% go-forward."
- **Q1-FY26 (Jun-2025) — tariffs enter, story holds.** Management introduces a "$135 million... unmitigated tariff impact" and a 4-warehouse / 150-person cost program. Donna Karan grows "nearly 50%." Sonia Rykiel relaunch *cancelled* — first concrete retrenchment. Pricing-power narrative gets heavy airtime ("treasury waiting to be spent").
- **Q2-FY26 (Sep-2025) — the macro "perfect windstorm."** Goldfarb's own phrase. Gross margin pressured by "higher-than-expected tariff costs." India tariff hit ($30M FOB at risk). New license pipeline introduced as the *next* growth leg: **Converse (via Nike, global), BCBG, Marc Jacobs adjacency.** Net cash $286M; $25M buyback. The thesis quietly *widens* from "owned brands replace PVH" to "owned brands + a new licensing book replace PVH."
- **Q3-FY26 (Dec-2025) — the number that matters.** Management finally quantifies the hole: CK/Tommy "at its peak... over $1.5 billion... this year... approximately $800 million," and teams "replaced more than 70% of the lost sales volume." This is the single most important disclosed fact in the entire transcript set — it concedes a ~$700M peak-to-now revenue evaporation and claims 70% organic replacement. Net cash $174M.
- **Q4-FY26 (Mar-2026) — the trough is printed.** Full-year revenue $2.96B (down from $3.18B), non-GAAP EPS *$2.61* (vs $4.42 two years prior — a 41% cut), hit by a $17.5M Saks bad-debt charge ($0.30). Owned brands now "close to 60% of revenue." First-ever dividend ($0.10/qtr) declared. Converse/BCBG now shipping; Marc Jacobs acquisition (with WHP Global) signed as the new growth flywheel.

**Thesis evolution:** *not* a contraction and *not* clean expansion — it is a **pivot held together by serial substitution.** Each quarter a growth driver is introduced (owned brands → new licenses → acquisitions) precisely as the prior anchor (PVH) deflates. The honesty of the disclosure improves over time (the $800M and "70% replaced" admissions in Q3 are franker than the proud Q4-FY25 framing), but the *direction* of the base business is unambiguously down.

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Mgmt claim | Quarter | The number | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Record non-GAAP EPS of $4.42, +9%" | Q4-FY25 | GAAP EPS trajectory: FY25 $4.20 → **FY26 $1.51** (-64%); non-GAAP FY26 $2.61 | **Richer than reality.** The non-GAAP record was real but it was the *last* good year; the very next year EPS was cut nearly in half. The "+9%" was peak-narrative. |
| Owned brands "replaced more than 70% of the lost [PVH] sales volume" | Q3-FY26 | Revenue: FY25 $3.18B → FY26 $2.96B (-7%); 3-yr CAGR **-2.9%** | **Half-true and self-flattering.** 70% replacement of a *shrinking* line still nets to negative growth. Revenue is down 7% YoY and has compounded *negatively* for three years. "Replaced 70%" is a true sentence that obscures a falling top line. |
| Donna Karan "~40% growth," "$1 billion potential" | every call | Not separately disclosed ("we don't disclose segmented pieces"). Total revenue still -7% | **Unverifiable and undisclosed.** Management explicitly refuses to size Donna Karan. A 40%-growing brand on an undisclosed (likely <$300M) base cannot offset a $700M PVH hole — the math is in the consolidated decline. Pattern: loud about the winner, mute on its scale. |
| "Strong financial position," net cash, capital return | Q3/Q4-FY26 | Net cash recomputed **+$101M** (cash $394M > debt $294M); $54M returned FY26; Altman-Z **3.8** (safe); Piotroski **8/9** | **TRUE and the strongest fact in the file.** This is genuinely high-quality. Net cash, $900M liquidity, 8/9 Piotroski, real buyback + new dividend. The balance sheet is not the risk. |
| Gross margin "stable... will normalize and expand" | Q2-Q4-FY26 | Gross margin **38.4% (stable)**; net margin collapsed to **2.3%** | **Mixed.** Gross margin genuinely held (~38.4-38.6%) — credible, owned-brand mix is accretive. But *net* margin fell to 2.3% because tariffs ($65M FY26 unmitigated) + bad-debt + SG&A deleverage on a falling top line crushed the operating line. Gross-margin resilience is real; bottom-line economics are not yet. |
| Pricing power "treasury waiting to be spent" | Q1-Q2-FY26 | EPS -64% YoY, net margin 2.3% | **Not yet evidenced in the P&L.** The pricing-power story is asserted repeatedly but the consolidated margins show the company *absorbing* tariff costs ("absorbing a portion... to remain competitive"), not commanding price. Aspirational, not yet banked. |
| FY27 EPS guide | Q4-FY26 / Q1-FY27 | **Non-GAAP $2.00-$2.10** (down 19-23%) ex-Marc-Jacobs; headline $3.85-3.95 *only because* of a one-time **$102.7M IEEPA tariff refund** | **Critical tell.** The underlying earnings power for FY27 is guided *down again* to ~$2.05. The TTM EPS of $2.83 and the headline FY27 number are flattered by a non-recurring tariff refund — strip it and the run-rate is ~$2.00, not $2.83. The screen's "fwd EPS growth +11.3%" is a refund artifact. |

**Net forensic read:** the balance sheet claims check out cleanly; the *earnings-power* claims are systematically richer than the consolidated P&L justifies, because management narrates the growing parts (Donna Karan, owned mix) and stays silent on their scale while the shrinking part (PVH) drives the reported decline. The single most important correction the numbers force: **normalized FY27 EPS is ~$2.00-2.10, not the $2.83 TTM or $3.90 headline** — both are inflated by a one-time tariff refund.

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: DECLINING from a high base, stabilizing defensive by Q4.** Goldfarb is a congenitally promotional CEO, so absolute tone reads bullish throughout; the *relative* shift is what matters.

- **Q4-FY25 (offensive):** "remarkable top-line growth," "world-class teams," "epic relaunch," "single biggest launch." Pure offense.
- **Q1-FY26 (offense + first hedge):** still "cautiously optimistic," but introduces "$135 million... unmitigated tariff impact," cancels Sonia Rykiel, and pivots into cost-program language (warehouses, headcount, lease kickouts).
- **Q2-FY26 (defensive peak):** *"If there was a perfect windstorm, we were hit with it"* — Goldfarb's own framing concedes a siege. "Prioritizing margin over sales," "absorbing a portion of these costs." This is the most defensive call.
- **Q3-FY26 (defensive-stabilizing):** frank quantification ("$800M," "70% replaced"), "prudent approach to our outlook," but cold-weather tailwind and full-price mix give him a real win to point to ("women's outerwear... up nearly 20%").
- **Q4-FY26 (re-offense on a lower base):** dividend initiation + Marc Jacobs/Converse/BCBG = forward-looking again, but explicitly "primarily maintaining the talent pool," cost savings "rolled into fiscal 2028" — i.e., no near-term leverage.

**Guidance specificity** *increased* over time (they began quantifying tariff dollars, the PVH hole, segment growth rates) — a credibility positive. The shift from offensive (growth/investment) to defensive (efficiency/cost/macro) language is clear and real between Q4-FY25 and Q2-FY26, then partially reverses as new licenses give them something to sell again.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY — Score: **Medium-High**

The earnings-beat rate is **6/7 quarters (86%)**, and the transcripts corroborate a *sandbagging* pattern: every FY26 quarter "exceeded the high end of our guidance" or came in "well above the top end of our guidance range" (Q1, Q2, Q3 all explicitly). Management beats its own EPS guide reliably.

**But the beats are quality-mixed:**
- They consistently beat on *earnings* via cost discipline and full-price mix — genuine operational delivery.
- They consistently *missed on sales* vs the original FY26 plan (Hudson's Bay liquidation, Saks bankruptcy, India tariff $30M, Sonia Rykiel cancellation) — the top line repeatedly came in light to external shocks, with EPS rescued below the line.
- The "excluding the impact of Saks bankruptcy, full year EPS would have exceeded the high end of our guidance" (Q4-FY26) is a classic *ex-items* beat claim — credible here (Saks was a genuine exogenous bankruptcy) but it is the second consecutive year a major-customer Chapter 11 (Hudson's Bay, then Saks) dinged results, which raises a channel-concentration question management does not volunteer.

**Reliability: Medium-High.** They deliver the EPS number they guide to and are honest about the *shocks*. The caveat is that the EPS line is increasingly an *engineered* number (cost-out, mix, one-time tariff refund) sitting on a structurally declining revenue base — so "they hit guidance" is true but tells you less about earnings *power* than it appears.

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **"How big is Donna Karan?"** (Serna, Telsey, repeatedly across Q4-FY25 → Q4-FY26). Management *categorically refuses* every time: "We don't disclose... the segmented pieces." This is the single most-deflected question. Analysts are pushing because the entire bull case rests on owned-brand scale, and management's refusal to size the hero brand is the central informational asymmetry. The closest tell: in Q4-FY26 Goldfarb says Donna Karan in 18 months is "bigger and further along than... Calvin Klein" was — implying meaningful but still sub-CK scale (CK grew "to $1.2 billion").
- **Tariff mitigation %** (Serna, Q1-FY26): "how much do you expect to mitigate out of the $135 million?" Management non-answered ("we did not feel comfortable specifying"). It later turned into a real $65M unmitigated FY26 hit — the analyst's worry was vindicated.
- **Pricing resistance** (Kearney, Q2-FY26): "are we starting to push against the limit of what is possible on price?" Goldfarb conceded "we are seeing some resistance" from *retailers* (not consumers) — a crack in the pricing-power story that management talks around.
- **Inventory/promotion risk** (Kearney, Q1-Q2): industry inventory build → promotional risk. Management deflected ("not feeling very much pressure on promotions"). Partially vindicated by the off-price/full-price mix discussion.
- **What analysts worry about that management isn't addressing:** the *absolute earnings floor* once PVH fully rolls off. Management keeps the conversation on growth brands and never frames the through-trough EPS the way the FY27 ~$2.05 guide later forces.

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

**🔴 Red flags**
- 🔴 **EPS cut nearly in half** FY25 $4.20 → FY26 $1.51 (GAAP), non-GAAP $4.42 → $2.61. Revenue -2.9% 3-yr CAGR, -7% YoY. The base business is shrinking, full stop. (all FY26 calls)

- 🔴 **FY27 underlying EPS guided DOWN again to ~$2.00-2.10** ex-Marc-Jacobs; the headline $3.85-3.95 and TTM $2.83 are flattered by a non-recurring $102.7M IEEPA tariff refund. The screen's "+11.3% fwd EPS growth" is a refund mirage. (Q1-FY27)
- 🔴 **Insider selling** (open-market): 2 buys (28k sh) vs 10 sells (542k sh) — net heavy selling is the real conviction signal, not the grant-inflated 0.05 buy_ratio.
- 🔴 **Pricing power asserted but not banked** — net margin 2.3%, "absorbing a portion" of tariffs; retailer price resistance conceded Q2-FY26.

**🟢 Green flags**
- 🟢 **Fortress balance sheet:** net cash +$101M, ~$900M liquidity, Altman-Z 3.8, Piotroski 8/9. No solvency risk, full optionality. (Q3/Q4-FY26)
- 🟢 **Gross margin held ~38.4-38.6%** through a tariff war via owned-brand mix — the mix-shift thesis is *visibly* working at the gross line. (Q3/Q4-FY26)
- 🟢 **Owned brands 50% → ~60% of revenue in one year**, mid-single-digit organic growth offsetting most of the PVH cliff — the pivot is executing, not just narrated. (Q4-FY26)
- 🟢 **Capital return initiated:** first-ever dividend + $50M buyback at ~6x FCF — disciplined, accretive, owner-aligned. (Q3/Q4-FY26)
- 🟢 **Genuine self-help levers ahead:** retail segment to breakeven (+$14M), 4-warehouse consolidation, FY28 cost program, PVH drag *ending* — the worst of the revenue cliff is in the rear-view by FY27/28.
- 🟢 **Real beat discipline:** 6/7 quarters, every FY26 quarter above the high end of guidance.

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Terminology creep from "owned brands replace PVH" → "we're a brand builder/licensor."** By Q4-FY26 the self-description is "a brand builder and long-term steward of both our owned and licensed brands." The Converse/BCBG/Marc Jacobs additions show management quietly re-acknowledging that *licensing* (the model PVH just burned them on) remains structurally core — they are not actually exiting third-party-brand dependence, they are *re-sourcing* it (Nike/Converse, WHP/Marc Jacobs). The PVH lesson (a licensor can pull the rug) is not fully internalized.
- **Capital allocation pivot is the loudest non-verbal signal.** A company that has historically hoarded cash for acquisitions initiated a *dividend* (Q3-FY26) — that is management implicitly conceding the reinvestment runway is narrower than the "$1B Donna Karan" rhetoric implies, OR signaling confidence in a durable cash floor. Combined with the Marc Jacobs deal, it reads as "we'll do both, opportunistically" — but the dividend is the first admission that not every dollar has a high-return home.
- **"Margin over sales" (Q2-FY26)** is a quiet regime change for an apparel company — they are willing to shrink the top line to protect the P&L. Defensible, but it caps the upside surprise on revenue.
- **Marc Jacobs structure** (100% of OpCo, 50% of IP JV with WHP Global) shows Goldfarb has learned *something* from PVH — he now wants ownership of IP, not just a license. A genuine, if late, strategic upgrade.
- **CEO key-man risk unspoken:** Morris Goldfarb founded and still runs the company; the entire promotional, deal-driven culture is him. Not discussed, but a real terminal-value variable for a family-controlled small-cap.

---

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING (stabilizing) — a self-help transition off a melting licensed annuity onto an owned-brand base, executed on a fortress balance sheet but onto a structurally smaller, lower-multiple revenue pool.**

**The single most important thing to weigh before sizing:** the catalyst here is *not* a hard-dated event — it is the **multi-year completion of the PVH wind-down and the proof that owned-brand growth can re-inflate EPS above the ~$2.00-2.10 FY27 trough.** The danger is anchoring on the TTM $2.83 EPS or the $3.90 headline guide, both of which are flattered by a one-time tariff refund; the *clean* run-rate is ~$2.05, which at $34.86 is **~17x trough earnings, not 5.9x P/FCF** as the screen's optics suggest. The +71% DCF MoS is the most aggressive model and the EPS series it extrapolates is the inflated one. The genuine value support is the net-cash + ~6x *FCF* + owned-brand mix improving — a real but *un-catalyzed*, self-paced re-rate.

**MOAT:** intangible-brand — but **NARROW**. G-III owns real brand equity in DKNY/Donna Karan/Karl Lagerfeld, yet the model is wholesale-dependent (91% of revenue), department-store-channel-exposed (two customer bankruptcies in two years), and historically reliant on *licensed* third-party brands it does not control (the PVH amputation is the proof of fragility). ROIC 8.8% / ROE 6.1% are mid-tier and *fell* hard (EPS -64%) — not the signature of a wide moat. Owned-brand mix is *improving* the moat at the margin (gross margin holding, accretive licensing income), so the trend is **STABLE-to-modestly-widening on quality even as the base shrinks** — net read **STABLE**, not eroding (the decline is a forced license roll-off, not a margin/returns collapse from competition).

**SECULAR THREAT:** the relevant structural force is **retail-channel-shift** (e-commerce/DTC eroding the department-store wholesale channel that is 91% of G-III's revenue). This is **material, not terminal:** department-store wholesale is in secular slow decline (the Hudson's Bay/Saks bankruptcies are symptoms), which caps the terminal multiple and is a real headwind — but G-III is *actively migrating* toward DTC, owned-brand, full-price and licensing income, and the brands themselves are not obsolete. It is a value-cap, not a value-destroyer. The cheapness is therefore **a fear-print over a genuine but slow transition, NOT a melting-ice-cube** — provided the owned-brand engine keeps offsetting the channel drag.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: PIVOTING | MOAT: NARROW | MOAT_TREND: STABLE | SECULAR_THREAT: material

bull_thesis:
At around $32, G-III trades at roughly 6x price-to-free-cash-flow (the share price divided by the cash the business actually generates, here trailing free cash flow of $168M), as if it were a melting ice cube. The bull view is that the cube is reshaping, not melting. The bear case is almost entirely about one event: the loss of the Calvin Klein and Tommy Hilfiger licenses, which belong to PVH. Those brands peaked at more than $1.5B in wholesale sales and roll down to about $800M in the fiscal year ending early 2026 and about $330M in the following fiscal year (a roughly $470M giveback already built into the $2.71B sales guide). That following year is the low point: management's guidance for adjusted earnings per share of $2.15 to $2.25 absorbs essentially all of the remaining PVH hit. After that, the headwind is mostly gone and the brands G-III owns outright can compound against a clean base.

That owned-brand engine is real and measurable. Owned brands grew to about 60% of revenue, up from about 50%. Donna Karan grew roughly 40%, reached about 1,900 points of sale, saw donnakaran.com traffic up 170%, and added about 100,000 new customers (management called it the single biggest launch the company has ever tackled, and has repeatedly pointed to $1 billion in net-sales potential). Karl Lagerfeld grew in the high single digits. Gross margin (revenue left after the direct cost of the goods) is structurally improving, with management guiding to as much as 300 basis points (3 percentage points) of expansion, because owned brands pay no licensing royalty.

The balance sheet is genuinely strong: net cash of about $101M (more cash than debt) and more than $900M in total liquidity. That funds a $0.10 dividend plus about $50M of buybacks, and also the roughly $500M Marc Jacobs joint venture, a multi-decade license on a globally recognized brand that turns profitable for G-III after the first 12 months. Guidance has been highly credible (beating expectations in six of the last seven quarters, with detailed bridges for tariffs, warehouse costs, and earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization). A one-time $102.7M tariff refund under the IEEPA program, following a Supreme Court ruling, added cash in the first quarter of the new fiscal year.

If owned brands plus Marc Jacobs, Converse, and BCBG hold earnings near the roughly $2.20 floor and then re-grow toward $2.75 to $3.00 by the 2028-2029 fiscal years on a clean base, an 8-to-9x earnings multiple on $2.75, plus the net cash and the value of the Marc Jacobs option, supports a fair value above today's price. This holds only if four things prove out: that the coming fiscal year is genuinely the earnings low point; that Donna Karan keeps growing 30%-plus without giving back margin; that Marc Jacobs turns profitable on schedule without over-borrowing; and that no further large customers go bankrupt.
bear_thesis:
This is a structurally disadvantaged wholesale apparel distributor whose best year just ended and whose competitive protection was just shown to be illusory. PVH simply revoked the Calvin Klein and Tommy Hilfiger licenses that were more than half of sales two years ago, and a $250M lawsuit (G-III suing PVH for breach of contract plus a court declaration that it owes no minimum royalties) confirms the split is adversarial, not orderly.

The financials are deteriorating, not bottoming on a hidden growth story. Revenue fell 7% in the fiscal year ending early 2026 and is guided down another roughly 8% to $2.71B in the next year. Reported earnings per share collapsed from $4.20 to $1.51, a 64% drop. Net margin is a razor-thin 2.3%. Return on equity (annual profit as a share of shareholders' money) is 6.1%, and return on invested capital is 8.8% and falling, the mark of an eroding franchise, not a widening one.

The owned-brand engine is told as a growth story, but management refuses to size Donna Karan (it does not disclose the figure), so the load-bearing number cannot be verified, and even with roughly 40% Donna Karan growth the total top line still shrank. Distribution is structurally exposed to the shift away from department stores: both Hudson's Bay and Saks went bankrupt within the window (Saks alone meant $17.5M of bad debt and a $0.30 hit to earnings per share). The new growth seeds carry the same dependency that just failed. As founder-CEO Goldfarb said of Converse: where the company has less control, it does not set the rules; it is the servant to the licensor.

The net cash the bulls prize is being spent: the roughly $500M Marc Jacobs deal (paid in cash plus a draw on the revolving credit line, and a drag on profit for the first 12 months) pushes the company toward net debt right as the business bottoms and integrates a turnaround brand. Insiders are net sellers in the open market (2 purchases of 28,000 shares against 10 sales of 542,000 shares). The $102.7M first-quarter beat was a one-time tariff refund; without it, the quarter was an adjusted loss of $0.21 per share. Live analyst price targets have come down to roughly $26 to $29, versus the stale $39 in the model. Cheap on price-to-free-cash-flow is the classic value trap when the cash-flow base is shrinking, the competitive protection is eroding, and the cash cushion is being deployed into an unproven acquisition. The bear case is confirmed if earnings for the coming year print below the $2.15 floor, if Donna Karan growth decelerates below 25%, or if Marc Jacobs slips its closing date or guides to larger dilution.
consensus_delta:
For a deep-value name, the question is whether the market is pricing terminal decline into a company with stable or growing earnings power. Here it is not, so where we disagree with the market is small. The model carries a $39.33 target (about 13% upside) and a discounted-cash-flow fair value of $121; both are stale extrapolations off pre-cliff numbers. Live analyst targets have come down to roughly $26 to $29, meaning the market already sees fair value below today's $32 print.

The genuine disagreement is small and cuts both ways. On one side, bulls under-credit that the coming fiscal year is a clean earnings low point and that owned brands compound off a smaller, higher-margin base once the PVH roll-off completes in December 2026. On the other, bears under-credit the roughly $500M of Marc Jacobs cash spending and dilution, and the adversarial $250M PVH lawsuit. On balance, there is no large mispriced asymmetry here; the market is not looking the wrong way, it has already marked the cliff. The one real edge a patient holder has is that the coming year's optical earnings collapse (and the noise from the one-time $102.7M tariff refund) may overshoot to the downside into the trough, but that is a reason to wait for a wash-out, not a live edge today.
forcing_function: There is no hard, dated event that forces the stock to re-rate, which is the core problem for a book that looks for catalysts. The dates that do exist mark mechanical decline, not asymmetry: the last major Calvin Klein and Tommy Hilfiger licenses expire on December 31, 2026 (the PVH roll-off completes, a slow grind rather than a pop); the roughly $500M Marc Jacobs venture closes around the third quarter of the new fiscal year (a capital-deployment event that drags on profit in its first year, not a value unlock); and the $250M PVH lawsuit is a multi-year, all-or-nothing case with no fixed timeline. There is no activist investor, no spin-off, no asset sale, and no buyout offer. The only thing that would force a positive re-rating is operational: two or three quarters of clean, post-PVH owned-brand growth stabilizing earnings above the roughly $2.20 floor in the 2028 fiscal year, which is a show-me, not a date.
valley_of_death: The next 3 to 9 months (the risky window just ahead) are the cash-and-earnings low point, not the recovery. First, the first quarter already guided to and printed an adjusted loss of about $0.21 per share once you strip out the one-time tariff refund, and full-year adjusted earnings are guided to just $2.15 to $2.25, the lowest in years, as the final roughly $470M of PVH revenue rolls off. Second, the roughly $500M Marc Jacobs deal closes around the third quarter, funded by cash plus the revolving credit line and explicitly a drag on profit for the first 12 months; it consumes the net-cash cushion exactly when earnings are weakest and adds integration risk on a turnaround brand. Third, the shift away from department stores keeps producing landmines: after Hudson's Bay and Saks, any further department-store bankruptcy is a direct hit from bad debt and lost shipments. Fourth, tariff mitigation is unfinished and the tariff refund does not recur, so the following year laps a one-time gain. The stock can stay pinned or fall into the trough before any owned-brand-led re-acceleration shows up in the consolidated numbers (the 2028 fiscal year and beyond).
positioning_washout: There is limited forced-selling pressure here; this is not a dividend-cut or index-removal setup. The $0.10 dividend is tiny (not a yield-focused shareholder base to flush out) and was only just started. There is no debt covenant wall, because the company holds net cash today. The cleaner wash-out would be driven by sentiment and earnings: the coming year's optical earnings collapse plus the dilutive Marc Jacobs close could flush out momentum and quality-focused holders and tax-loss sellers into the trough, and the small size of the company (about $1.4B market value, roughly 44M shares) means that selling moves the price. Insiders are already net sellers in the open market (10 sales of 542,000 shares against 2 purchases of 28,000 shares), so there is no internal buying to backstop a flush. A genuine capitulation (below $25, into the coming year's trough prints) is the level where the value case would actually become asymmetric.
moderator_conclusion:
Our call is to pass for now, with low conviction. Applying the June 2026 market backdrop: it rewards hard, dated catalysts in a favorable window and structural special situations in deep, lightly-covered corners of the market (distressed and deleveraging names ahead of spin-offs, ahead of forced sellers), and it penalizes slow operational grinds with no clear catalyst. G-III is none of the favored types; it is a forced operational turnaround of an eroding wholesale-apparel franchise, financed by spending its balance-sheet cushion (the roughly $500M Marc Jacobs deal) into the earnings trough.

The load-bearing catalyst (the PVH wind-down plus owned-brand re-growth) is a dated but soft one, with no contractual payout: the PVH roll-off is a mechanically-dated slow grind the market has already marked, the dividend has already happened, and Marc Jacobs is a dilutive use of capital rather than a value unlock. The apparent discount to peers is largely shared-factor sector pressure from the shift away from department stores (the whole licensed-apparel group is re-rating down together), not name-specific edge, so we do not credit it. The forensic review is honest (management credibility is high, guidance is reliable, the margin-mix improvement is real, and Donna Karan is a genuinely impressive asset), which keeps this from being an outright avoid, but honest management of a shrinking, eroding base is not an edge.

At about $32 the stock sits at or above our base-case sum-of-the-parts fair value (valuing each business line separately) of about $27, with a 31% drop to the bear case and only a contingent, dilution-gated 19% to the bull case. Separately, our value-only conviction is also low (well under the moat-aware ceiling): on pure valuation versus sum-of-the-parts and forensic quality, ignoring catalysts and backdrop, the optical cheapness of roughly 6x price-to-free-cash-flow is offset by an eroding competitive position (return on invested capital of 8.8% and falling, net margin of 2.3%, earnings per share down 64%), a material long-term threat from the shift away from department stores, a shrinking base for the coming year, and a cash cushion being deployed into an unproven acquisition; a low multiple on a structurally shrinking, channel-pressured base has the flavor of a value trap, not value. The right action is to keep this on a watchlist for a wash-out below $25 into the coming year's trough; today it is a pass.
sop_breakdown: Our base case of about $27 per share is a trough-and-recover earnings value plus a discounted overlay for cash and options. Start with the earnings core: normalize adjusted earnings per share one notch above the coming year's $2.15 to $2.25 trough to about $2.50 (a partial owned-brand recovery and 300 basis points, or 3 percentage points, of margin-mix improvement, but haircut for channel decay and execution risk), times a de-rated but still quality apparel multiple of about 9x, which gives roughly $22.50 per share. Add the net-cash overlay: the roughly $101M of net cash (about $2.30 per share) is only half-credited (about $1.15 per share) because the roughly $500M Marc Jacobs deal is set to consume it and tip the company toward net debt, so it is a probability-weighted add-back, not a clean one. Add the Marc Jacobs option, a small positive of about $2 per share net of first-year dilution and integration risk, and the retail segment at about $1 per share on a breakeven path. This reconciles the $38 bull case (a free-cash-flow framing that treats the trough as temporary) and the $22 bear case (trough earnings times a de-rated multiple, with the cash spent) toward about $27. As a cross-check against peers: the peer file anchors on Ralph Lauren at 34x and PVH at about 10x, but Ralph Lauren is a premium owned-brand house and the wrong comparison; the de-rated licensing and wholesale group (PVH around 10x, Hanesbrands and Oxford Industries around 7 to 9x) supports about 9x, consistent with the $27 base.
risk_reward: The trade-off is roughly even to poor at about $32. The drop to the bear case (around $22) is about 31%. The move up to base-case fair value (around $27) is actually negative from today's $32 print, because the stock is modestly above base-case fair value. The move up to the bull case (around $38) is about 19%, but it requires the clean trough-and-recover path and a non-dilutive Marc Jacobs. The asymmetry is unfavorable: the stock is priced at or above the base case, with a 31% downside and only a contingent 19% upside. This is not an attractive entry.
peer_comps_note: The peer file's verdict that the stock is cheap versus peers rests partly on a stale Ralph Lauren anchor of 34x. Ralph Lauren is a premium direct-to-consumer owned-brand house and the wrong comparison for G-III's licensing and wholesale mix. The right anchor is the current, de-rated group: PVH at about 10x forward price-to-earnings, and Hanesbrands and Oxford Industries at about 7 to 9x. On that basis, G-III at roughly 6x price-to-free-cash-flow (or about 14 to 15x trough adjusted earnings per share) is roughly fairly to cheaply valued, not a screaming discount. Crucially, the apparel-wholesale group is re-rating down together on the shift away from department stores plus tariff drag (PVH itself, VF Corp, and Capri are all distressed or below 10x), so the gap to Ralph Lauren is largely shared-factor sector pressure (channel decay, department-store decline), not anything specific to G-III. Do not credit the Ralph Lauren discount as a name-specific edge.
interrogator_dossier:
# G-III Apparel Group (GIII) — a deep dive on management and the setup

*Based on five consecutive earnings calls (the fiscal-2025 fourth quarter on 2025-03-13 through the fiscal-2026 fourth quarter on 2026-03-12), cross-referenced against the screener's financial history. Catalyst status updated with live web verification to June 2026.*

## 1. The story over time

The story across these five calls is a single, consistent arc: a forced, multi-year amputation of the Calvin Klein and Tommy Hilfiger (PVH) licensed business, with management racing to re-grow a portfolio of brands it owns outright fast enough to fill the hole. This is not a growth story management chose; it is a managed decline they are trying to convert into a transformation.

- **Opening posture (fiscal-2025 Q4, 2025-03-13):** Confident and on offense. Goldfarb leads with "record non-GAAP earnings per share of $4.42 in fiscal 2025, an increase of 9%," and frames the PVH roll-off as already absorbed: Calvin Klein and Tommy Hilfiger "now collectively represent approximately 34% of our total sales, down from over 50% two years ago, and we expect a further decline to approximately 25% by the end of fiscal 2026." The growth engine introduced here is the Donna Karan relaunch ("the single biggest launch... we've ever tackled," "$1 billion in annual reported net sales potential," "approaches 40% go-forward") plus DKNY, Karl Lagerfeld, Vilebrequin, and the roughly 20% AWWG stake for European reach.
- **Mid-arc (fiscal-2026 Q1 through Q3, 2025-06 through 2025-12):** Two new headwinds bolt on. First, tariffs: a "$135 million" unmitigated fiscal-2026 impact, China cut to "<20% of production," the Sonia Rykiel launch cancelled, and India product ($30M FOB) potentially abandoned. Second, customer bankruptcies: Hudson's Bay (Canada) liquidation, then Saks. The owned-brand drumbeat intensifies (Donna Karan "nearly 50%" growth in Q1; donnakaran.com traffic "up approximately 150%" in Q3) but is increasingly used to offset, not add.
- **Closing posture (fiscal-2026 Q4, 2026-03-12):** The transformation is now explicit and the base is visibly smaller. Calvin Klein and Tommy Hilfiger peaked at ">$1.5 billion in annual net wholesale sales" and "this year... approximately $800 million"; owned brands are "close to 60% of revenue this year, up from roughly 50%." New growth seeds are introduced late and unproven: Converse (via Nike), BCBG, and Marc Jacobs-adjacent licensing ambitions. A $0.10 quarterly dividend and roughly $50M of buybacks were started, a capital-return pivot consistent with a maturing, lower-growth profile.

**Quietly dropped or softened:** Sonia Rykiel (killed). AWWG (loud in fiscal-2025 Q4, near-silent by fiscal-2026 Q4). The "$1B Donna Karan" and "$1.2B like-Calvin-Klein" comparisons are repeated as aspiration but never sized; management explicitly does not disclose the size of the business.

## 2. Claims versus the financials

| Management claim | Quarter | The hard numbers | How it checks out |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Record non-GAAP EPS of $4.42, +9%" | FY25 Q4 | Screener shows fiscal-2025 (Jan-2025) net income $194M, GAAP EPS $4.20; fiscal-2026 EPS collapses to **$1.51** (-64% year over year); non-GAAP fiscal-2026 **$2.61** | True for fiscal-2025, but the peak was the last good year; EPS halved the very next year |
| Owned brands offset PVH roll-off | all | Revenue: fiscal-2025 $3.18B then **fiscal-2026 $2.96B (-7%)**; 3-year growth rate -2.9% | Partly true; owned-brand growth slowed the bleed but did not offset it, and the top line is shrinking |
| "Donna Karan ~40% growth, $1B potential" | FY26 Q4 | Never sized; "we don't disclose the segmented pieces." Total revenue still fell 7% | Cannot be verified on its own; a real bright spot inside a shrinking whole |
| Gross margin "stable / expanding" | all | Gross margin **38.4% (stable)**; fiscal-2026 Q3 38.6%; management guides "up to 300bps" expansion next year | Credible on margin mix; owned brands carry no royalty, the one clean structural positive |
| "Strong balance sheet, net cash" | FY26 Q4 | Screener live-correct: **net cash ~$101M** (cash $394M against debt $294M); >$900M total liquidity | True and genuine, a real value add-back, until Marc Jacobs (see section 6) |
| Beat guidance "6/7 qtrs (86%)" | — | Confirmed by the call pattern ("exceeded the high end" recurs) | True, but the beats are off a declining base, so the quality of the beat is low |
| Free-cash-flow strength, price-to-free-cash-flow 5.9x | — | Trailing free cash flow **$168M**; net margin only **2.3%**; return on equity 6.1%, **return on invested capital 8.8% and falling** | Mixed; free cash flow looks cheap, but returns are thin and deteriorating |

The narrative is richer than the trajectory justifies on growth (the owned-brand story is told as a growth engine, while the consolidated numbers are a managed decline) and honest on margin and balance sheet (the two places the story actually matches the evidence).

## 3. Tone and confidence over time

**Trajectory: stable to defensive (high, constant confidence, but the content shifted from offense to defense).**

- Fiscal-2025 Q4 (on offense): "remarkable top-line growth," "epic relaunch," "the single biggest launch... we've ever tackled."
- Fiscal-2026 Q1 (first defensive pivot): tariffs dominate, "potential unmitigated tariff impact... approximately $135 million"; "we made the decision to cancel production and postpone the launch [Sonia Rykiel]"; "exit of 4 warehouses and... over 150 people."
- Fiscal-2026 Q2: "prioritizing margin over sales"; "we're absorbing a portion of these costs."
- Fiscal-2026 Q3 through Q4: confidence reasserts on execution ("excellent year," "rock solid," "coming out... from a financial point of view, we're in great shape") but the vocabulary is now about efficiency, cost, and discipline, not expansion.

Guidance is consistently specific (quantified tariff dollars, warehouse and headcount counts, points of sale, earnings bridges), a credibility positive even as the message turned defensive.

## 4. How reliable is guidance

**High on delivery, but on a falling base.** The recurring "exceeded the high end of our guidance" pattern is backed by the screener's 86% beat rate. Specific delivered-versus-promised items check out: the fiscal-2026 Q1 "North American Retail... expected to breakeven this year" was tracking (losses "cut in half," then a breakeven path); tariff mitigation was quantified and revisited each quarter; the Saks hit ($17.5M bad debt, $0.30 per share) was disclosed cleanly rather than buried. The credibility gap is not about honesty; it is that management guides reliably to a number that keeps stepping down (next year's adjusted earnings of roughly $2.15 to $2.25 versus fiscal-2025's $4.42). They sandbag modestly and beat; the problem is the trend line, not the beat.

## 5. Where analysts pressed

- **Sizing Donna Karan:** Mauricio Serna (UBS) and Dana Telsey repeatedly press for the dollar size of Donna Karan and owned brands; management consistently deflects, "We don't disclose... the size of the business." This is the single most-deflected topic, and it matters because the entire bull case rests on an undisclosed number.
- **A ceiling on pricing power:** Paul Kearney (Barclays) pushes on whether they are "pushing against the limit of what is possible on price." Goldfarb concedes "we are seeing some resistance" (fiscal-2026 Q2), a rare admission that the pricing-power claim has a limit, gated by the off-price channel needing department-store prices for validation.
- **Tariff mitigation:** Analysts repeatedly ask how much of the $135M they will recover; management "did not feel comfortable specifying," a genuine non-answer that later resolved partly via the one-time tariff refund, not operating mitigation.
- **Converse and new licenses:** Robert Drbul probes Converse; Goldfarb's answer is strikingly hedged, "Where we have less control, we don't rule. We're guided by the licensor... It's hard to tell you where the brand goes." Candor is a positive sign, but it is a warning on the dependability of the new growth seeds.

## 6. Warning signs and bright spots

Warning signs:
- **EPS halved in one year** ($4.20 to $1.51 GAAP from fiscal-2025 to fiscal-2026, down 64%); the PVH cliff is real and front-loaded.
- **Top line shrinking** (-7% in fiscal-2026, with the next year guided to about $2.71B, a further roughly $470M PVH giveback); the owned-brand engine is not yet large enough to fill the hole.
- **Customer-concentration and channel fragility:** Hudson's Bay and Saks bankruptcies both hit inside the window; wholesale-to-department-store distribution is structurally exposed to channel decay.
- **Insider conviction is negative:** in the open market, 2 purchases (28,000 shares) against 10 sales (542,000 shares).
- **The net-cash thesis is about to invert:** the roughly $101M net cash and more than $900M liquidity is the valuation's best asset, but the roughly $500M Marc Jacobs deal (signed 2026-05-14, closing around the third quarter of the new fiscal year, a drag on profit for the first 12 months) consumes the cash and draws on the revolving credit line. The balance-sheet flexibility the bulls cite is being spent.

Bright spots:
- **Gross-margin mix genuinely improving:** owned brands carry no royalty; "up to 300bps" of expansion next year is structurally credible.
- **Donna Karan is a real, fast-scaling asset** (roughly 40% growth, about 1,900 points of sale, dotcom traffic up 170%, about 100,000 new customers), the one part of the valuation that is a true growth option.
- **Capital return started** ($0.10 dividend plus about $50M of buybacks), disciplined for a transition year.
- **Guidance reliably beaten** (86%) with quantified, specific guidance.

## 7. Reading between the lines

- **Vocabulary shifting from offense to defense:** "growth / launch / expansion" (fiscal-2025 Q4) became "mitigate / absorb / discipline / prioritize margin over sales / necessary evil... headcount reduction" (fiscal-2026). The tell is fiscal-2026 Q3's "if it doesn't work, we'll be profit-driven and not top-line driven," an explicit fallback to defense.
- **Appetite for acquisitions turned on:** in fiscal-2026 Q4, "our balance sheet supports our ability of funding a sizable acquisition." Six weeks later they signed the roughly $500M Marc Jacobs deal. The dividend and buyback were not so much a signal of capital-return discipline as a bridge before a large acquisition.
- **The lawsuit never surfaced on the calls:** management narrates the PVH exit as orderly, but the $250M lawsuit G-III filed against PVH (breach of contract plus a court declaration on minimum royalties) reveals the split is adversarial, not amicable, a fact entirely absent from the prepared remarks.
- **"We're the servant to the licensor" (Converse):** a rare structural admission that a meaningful slice of the new growth (Converse via Nike, BCBG) carries the same dependency that just cost them PVH.

## 8. Capital-allocation read (for the director)

**Classification: pivoting, under duress.** G-III is transforming from a licensed-brand wholesaler (PVH-dependent) into an owned-brand house, by necessity rather than from strength. The transformation is real and partly working (owned brands close to 60% of revenue, margin mix up, Donna Karan scaling), but it is a multi-year re-growth of a base that is shrinking now, financed by spending the balance-sheet flexibility on Marc Jacobs.

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** Is the owned-brand portfolio (Donna Karan, Karl Lagerfeld, Vilebrequin, DKNY, plus the new Marc Jacobs, Converse, and BCBG seeds) growing fast enough to outrun the roughly $470M PVH giveback next year and stabilize earnings near the roughly $2.15 to $2.25 adjusted floor, before the roughly $500M Marc Jacobs cash outflow and dilution remove the net-cash cushion? The cheapness (price-to-free-cash-flow around 6x) is only value if earnings bottom next year and re-grow; it is a value trap if the owned brands plateau and the new licenses (servant to the licensor) underwhelm.

**Economic moat: brand-based, narrow and eroding.** The owned brands (Donna Karan, Karl Lagerfeld) have genuine but young, undiversified brand equity; the wholesale-distribution and licensed-brand legs have no durable moat (proven by PVH's ability to simply revoke the licenses). The returns evidence says eroding: return on invested capital of 8.8% and falling, return on equity 6.1%, net margin 2.3%, EPS down 64% year over year, revenue shrinking, a franchise giving back returns from a high-water mark, not widening one. Moat trend: eroding.

**Long-term threat: material, not terminal.** The structural force is the shift away from department stores and wholesale distribution (the Hudson's Bay and Saks bankruptcies are the live evidence), which erodes G-III's primary path to market. It is material (it compounds the cyclical PVH hit and caps the long-run value of the wholesale business) but not terminal, because the owned brands give G-III a direct-to-consumer, digital, and global-licensing path the pure-wholesale legacy lacked. The cheapness is therefore a mix of a real fear-driven markdown (the PVH cliff is largely one-time) and a structurally-shrinking-base risk (channel decay plus licensed-brand non-ownership), not a clean mispricing.

Management credibility: high. Trajectory: pivoting. Moat: narrow and eroding. Moat trend: eroding. Long-term threat: material.
